4. Assimilation of Soil Moisture and Ocean Salinity (SMOS) brightness temperature into a large-scale distributed conceptual hydrological model to improve soil moisture predictions: the Murray–Darling basin in Australia as a test case (Hydrology and Earth System Sciences 2020, cites: 9)
   Abstract. The main objective of this study is to investigate how brightness temperature observations from satellite microwave sensors may help to reduce errors and uncertainties in soil moisture and evapotranspiration simulations with a large-scale conceptual hydro-meteorological model. In
addition, this study aims to investigate whether such a conceptual modelling framework, relying on parameter calibration, can reach the performance level of more complex physically based models for
soil moisture simulations at a large scale. We use the ERA-Interim publicly available forcing data set and couple the Community Microwave Emission Modelling (CMEM) platform radiative transfer model with a hydro-meteorological model to enable, therefore, soil moisture, evapotranspiration and brightness temperature simulations over the
Murray–Darling basin in Australia. The hydro-meteorological model is configured using recent developments in the SUPERFLEX framework, which enables tailoring the model structure to the specific needs of the application and to data availability and computational requirements. The hydrological model is first calibrated using only a sample of the Soil Moisture and Ocean Salinity (SMOS) brightness temperature observations (2010–2011). Next, SMOS brightness temperature observations are
sequentially assimilated into the coupled SUPERFLEX–CMEM model (2010–2015). For this experiment, a local ensemble transform Kalman filter is used. Our empirical results show that the SUPERFLEX–CMEM modelling chain is capable of predicting soil moisture at a performance level similar to that obtained for the same study area and with a quasi-identical experimental set-up using the Community Land Model (CLM) . This shows that a simple model, when calibrated using globally and freely available Earth observation data, can yield performance levels similar to those of a physically based (uncalibrated) model. The correlation between simulated and in situ observed soil moisture ranges from 0.62 to 0.72 for the surface and root zone soil moisture. The assimilation of SMOS brightness temperature observations into the SUPERFLEX–CMEM modelling chain improves the correlation between predicted and in situ observed surface and root zone soil moisture by 0.03 on average, showing improvements similar to those obtained using the CLM land surface model. Moreover, at the same time the assimilation improves the correlation between predicted and in situ observed monthly evapotranspiration by 0.02 on average.

1. Uncertainty Quantification Based on Conformal Prediction for Industrial Time Series With Distribution Shift (IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics 2025, cites: 7)
   Conformal prediction (CP) is known to theoretically guarantee prediction interval coverage under the exchangeability assumption. However, industrial time series collected from real-world industrial processes often violates this assumption due to temporal dependencies and distribution drift. Therefore, an uncertainty quantification framework is proposed for industrial time series, with the prediction interval composed of two one-sided intervals. Specifically, it adopts CP as the basic framework and integrates entire and local nonconformity score information to adjust the confidence levels of two one-tailed intervals over time. This enables the proposed method can adapt quickly to distribution shifts and provides effective prediction intervals. Two experiments show that the proposed method improves the efficiency of prediction intervals while guaranteeing coverage. Specifically, under a nominal confidence level 95%, the proposed method achieves an average empirical coverage of 95.0% with a 6.29% reduction in prediction interval width in the wastewater dataset. While in the actual sintering production dataset, it achieves a similar improvement, with a 95.6% coverage and a 15.70% reduction in width, compared to the best-performing benchmark model.

2. Adaptive Individual Uncertainty under Out-Of-Distribution Shift with Expert-Routed Conformal Prediction (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Reliable, informative, and individual uncertainty quantification (UQ) remains missing in current ML community. This hinders the effective application of AI/ML to risk-sensitive domains. Most methods either fail to provide coverage on new data, inflate intervals so broadly that they are not actionable, or assign uncertainties that do not track actual error, especially under a distribution shift. In high-stakes drug discovery, protein-ligand affinity (PLI) prediction is especially challenging as assay noise is heterogeneous, chemical space is imbalanced and large, and practical evaluations routinely involve distribution shift. In this work, we introduce a novel uncertainty quantification method, Trustworthy Expert Split-conformal with Scaled Estimation for Efficient Reliable Adaptive intervals (TESSERA), that provides per-sample uncertainty with reliable coverage guarantee, informative and adaptive prediction interval widths that track the absolute error. We evaluate on protein-ligand binding affinity prediction under both independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) and scaffold-based out-of-distribution (OOD) splits, comparing against strong UQ baselines. TESSERA attains near-nominal coverage and the best coverage-width trade-off as measured by the Coverage-Width Criterion (CWC), while maintaining competitive adaptivity (lowest Area Under the Sparsification Error (AUSE)). Size-Stratified Coverage (SSC) further confirms that intervals are right-sized, indicating width increases when data are scarce or noisy, and remain tight when predictions are reliable. By unifying Mixture of Expert (MoE) diversity with conformal calibration, TESSERA delivers trustworthy, tight, and adaptive uncertainties that are well-suited to selective prediction and downstream decision-making in the drug-discovery pipeline and other applications.

3. Dual Adaptive Conformal Prediction: Enhancing Coverage and Stability in Uncertainty Quantification (Design Automation Conference 2025, cites: 0)
   
 Conformal prediction (CP) is a framework that provides uncertainty quantification with valid marginal coverage for predictive models. Within statistical inference, split conformal prediction has been proposed as a basic method. Considering the shift of data distribution, adaptive CP method is presented as an improvement of the former methods. However, both CP methods cannot guarantee the expected coverage and precise coverage in practice, and the predicted interval results are unstable, providing ineffective uncertainty information. This method improves traditional split CP and split-adaptive CP methods by enhancing the adaptability of uncertainty estimation models, increasing their sensitivity to data variations, and improving the stability of prediction results. The experimental results show a more expected coverage and stronger stability of the proposed dual adaptive CP and provide inspiration for future research.

Using this result, we provide a method to design pseudo-calibrated sets that inflate the conformal threshold by a slack parameter to keep target coverage above a prescribed level. Finally, we propose a source-tuned pseudo-calibration algorithm that interpolates between hard pseudo-labels and randomized labels as a function of classifier uncertainty. Conf-Ot provides consistent relative improvements of up to 20% on set efficiency while being ×15 faster than popular transductive approaches. We make the code available 1.

   Conformal prediction is a distribution-free framework for uncertainty quantification that replaces point predictions with sets, offering marginal coverage guarantees (i.e., ensuring that the prediction sets contain the true label with a specified probability, in expectation). In this paper, we uncover a novel connection between conformal prediction and sparse softmax-like transformations, such as sparsemax and $\gamma$-entmax (with $\gamma>1$), which may assign nonzero probability only to a subset of labels. We introduce new non-conformity scores for classification that make the calibration process correspond to the widely used temperature scaling method. At test time, applying these sparse transformations with the calibrated temperature leads to a support set (i.e., the set of labels with nonzero probability) that automatically inherits the coverage guarantees of conformal prediction. Through experiments on computer vision and text classification benchmarks, we demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive results in terms of coverage, efficiency, and adaptiveness compared to standard non-conformity scores based on softmax.

3. Can bin-wise scaling improve consistency and adaptivity of prediction uncertainty for machine learning regression ? (arXiv.org 2023, cites: 4)
   Binwise Variance Scaling (BVS) has recently been proposed as a post hoc recalibration method for prediction uncertainties of machine learning regression problems that is able of more efficient corrections than uniform variance (or temperature) scaling. The original version of BVS uses uncertainty-based binning, which is aimed to improve calibration conditionally on uncertainty, i.e. consistency. I explore here several adaptations of BVS, in particular with alternative loss functions and a binning scheme based on an input-feature (X) in order to improve adaptivity, i.e. calibration conditional on X. The performances of BVS and its proposed variants are tested on a benchmark dataset for the prediction of atomization energies and compared to the results of isotonic regression.

4. Trustworthy Glucose Forecasting With Physiology-Constrained Neural Dynamics, Conformal Risk Control, and Risk-Sensitive Reinforcement Learning (IEEE Access 2026, cites: 0)
   Reliable short-horizon continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) forecasting is essential to prevent hypoglycemia, yet most data-driven models provide neither physiological awareness nor actionable safety guaranties. We introduce a trustworthy forecasting framework that unifies continuous-time neural dynamics, calibration-based conformal risk control (empirically validated in held-out subjects), and tail-risk-aware adaptation. First, a Neural Controlled Differential Equation (Neural CDE) encoder models irregular, multi-channel streams (CGM, insulin, carbohydrates, activity, time-of-day). A physiology regularizer anchors latent dynamics to plausible glucose responses (e.g., post-prandial rise, insulin-mediated decay) and penalizes simulator-inconsistent trends. Second, Conformal Risk Control calibrates prediction sets and provides an actionable deferral policy when uncertainty or low-glucose risk is high, achieving prediction sets calibrated to a nominal coverage target and empirically validated under subject-disjoint evaluation. Third, a risk-sensitive policy (CVaR-optimized PPO) adapts predictions to minimize tail errors, explicitly weighting under-predictions near hypoglycemia. We evaluated in BrisT1D and OhioT1DM with patient-wise splits and performed in-silico stress tests in the UVA/Padova simulator (meal/bolus perturbations, basal overrides, sensor noise). Across 30/60-minutes horizons, our method achieves MAE= 3.0–3.9 mg/dL, RMSE= 8.5–9.8 mg/dL, and MARD= 2.1–2.7%, outperforming strong baselines by 10–14 mg/dL RMSE and 10–13 mg/dL MAE on average. The risk control is calibrated at a nominal target of 90%, achieving 90.5 to 91.5% coverage with a average set width of 18-20 mg/dL and 5% to 6% deferral. Clinically, the rates of the SEG/Parkes A/B-zone increase by + 10–14 pp, and event-centric analyzes show PR-AUC= 0.92–0.95 and ROC-AUC= 0.94–0.96 with earlier warnings (9–11 min median lead-time) and a 35–45% reduction in hypoglycemia under-prediction near the threshold. To our knowledge, this is the first end-to-end framework combining physiology-constrained Neural CDEs, conformal risk control with deferral, and CVaR-PPO adaptation for CGM forecasting, delivering verifiable safety properties alongside state-of-the-art accuracy.

5. Event-Graph Neural Survival Model with Conformal Intervals for Predictive Maintenance in Industrial IoT (Scientia. Technology, Science and Society 2026, cites: 0)
   Predictive maintenance has become a fundamental component of Industry 4.0, driving both fault prediction and also optimized asset utilization in the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) scenarios. A deep learning model for remaining useful life (RUL) estimation and failure-risk prediction based on the survival analysis of turbofan engine degradation signals from the NASA C-MAPSS dataset is introduced in the context of this research. The time-to-failure (TTF) bins were selected to be discretized, and the model trained was one of the transformer-based architectures with combined temporal encoding and asset-specific embeddings that mapped survival probability distributions to the discretized TTF bins. The model scored highly and achieved a C-index of 0.83, an integrated Brier score of 0.60, and a mean absolute error of about 115 cycles on the FD001 test set. The conformal calibration framework produced approximately 92.5% empirical coverage with reliable prediction intervals. Furthermore, early-failure detection in a 10-bin horizon achieved AUROC = 0.91 and AUPRC = 0.87, thereby signaling strong sensitivity to upcoming failures. Survival curve visualization revealed interpretable degradation dynamics, and the predicted vs. true TTF plots showed very strong near-diagonal correlation. The event-graph transformer model proposed integrates uncertainty quantification, temporal degradation representation, and conformal reliability into a single powerful and trustworthy predictive maintenance approach for IIoT systems.

6. JUCAL: Jointly Calibrating Aleatoric and Epistemic Uncertainty in Classification Tasks (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   We study post-calibration uncertainty for trained ensembles of classifiers. Specifically, we consider both aleatoric (label noise) and epistemic (model) uncertainty. Among the most popular and widely used calibration methods in classification are temperature scaling (i.e., pool-then-calibrate) and conformal methods. However, the main shortcoming of these calibration methods is that they do not balance the proportion of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. Not balancing these uncertainties can severely misrepresent predictive uncertainty, leading to overconfident predictions in some input regions while being underconfident in others. To address this shortcoming, we present a simple but powerful calibration algorithm Joint Uncertainty Calibration (JUCAL) that jointly calibrates aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. JUCAL jointly calibrates two constants to weight and scale epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties by optimizing the negative log-likelihood (NLL) on the validation/calibration dataset. JUCAL can be applied to any trained ensemble of classifiers (e.g., transformers, CNNs, or tree-based methods), with minimal computational overhead, without requiring access to the models'internal parameters. We experimentally evaluate JUCAL on various text classification tasks, for ensembles of varying sizes and with different ensembling strategies. Our experiments show that JUCAL significantly outperforms SOTA calibration methods across all considered classification tasks, reducing NLL and predictive set size by up to 15% and 20%, respectively. Interestingly, even applying JUCAL to an ensemble of size 5 can outperform temperature-scaled ensembles of size up to 50 in terms of NLL and predictive set size, resulting in up to 10 times smaller inference costs. Thus, we propose JUCAL as a new go-to method for calibrating ensembles in classification.

7. Estimating Uncertainty in Multimodal Foundation Models using Public Internet Data (arXiv.org 2023, cites: 11)
   Foundation models are trained on vast amounts of data at scale using self-supervised learning, enabling adaptation to a wide range of downstream tasks. At test time, these models exhibit zero-shot capabilities through which they can classify previously unseen (user-specified) categories. In this paper, we address the problem of quantifying uncertainty in these zero-shot predictions. We propose a heuristic approach for uncertainty estimation in zero-shot settings using conformal prediction with web data. Given a set of classes at test time, we conduct zero-shot classification with CLIP-style models using a prompt template, e.g.,"an image of a", and use the same template as a search query to source calibration data from the open web. Given a web-based calibration set, we apply conformal prediction with a novel conformity score that accounts for potential errors in retrieved web data. We evaluate the utility of our proposed method in Biomedical foundation models; our preliminary results show that web-based conformal prediction sets achieve the target coverage with satisfactory efficiency on a variety of biomedical datasets.

8. Stability-aware forecasting of power-operation cost volatility under non-stationary data streams (International Conference on New Power System and Power Electronics 2026, cites: 0)
   Dynamic cost volatility forecasting for power-system operations is increasingly a streaming problem: operational conditions drift, variance bursts occur, and fixed offline models often degrade exactly when uncertainty is most consequential for stability-oriented decision support. This paper proposes a drift-aware forecasting algorithm for non-stationary power-operation cost streams that treats distribution shift as a controllable event rather than an unmodeled nuisance. The method constructs robust multi-scale representations from cost log-increments, maintains a latent volatility state for multi-step prediction, and performs event-driven online adaptation using a residual-discrepancy drift score with a trust-region projection to bound update instability. To make outputs decision-usable, we attach calibrated prediction intervals via online conformal quantiles that track the evolving residual scale, so alerting thresholds can be set with controlled risk under regime shifts. Empirically, the proposed approach improves long-horizon volatility forecasting and maintains near-nominal interval reliability on a large streaming benchmark, while stress tests with injected change points show faster recovery after abrupt shifts compared with strong transformer and state-space baselines. Ablation results further indicate that drift gating, bounded updates, and online calibration are each necessary to jointly achieve stable adaptation and dependable uncertainty for stability-oriented operation.

9. Multi-Task Deep Learning for Surface Metrology (Italian National Conference on Sensors 2025, cites: 0)
   A reproducible deep learning framework is presented for surface metrology to predict surface texture parameters together with their reported standard uncertainties. Using a multi-instrument dataset spanning tactile and optical systems, we jointly address measurement system type classification and regression of key surface parameters—arithmetic mean roughness (Ra), mean peak-to-valley roughness (Rz), and total roundness deviation (RONt)—alongside their reported standard uncertainties. Uncertainty is modelled via quantile and heteroscedastic regression heads, with post hoc conformal calibration used to obtain calibrated prediction intervals. On a held-out test set, high fidelity was achieved by single-target regressors (coefficients of determination: Ra 0.9824, Rz 0.9847, RONt 0.9918), with two uncertainty targets also well modelled (standard uncertainty of Ra 0.9899, standard uncertainty of Rz 0.9955); the standard uncertainty of RONt remained more difficult to learn (0.4934). The classifier reached 92.85% accuracy, and probability calibration was essentially unchanged after temperature scaling (expected calibration error 0.00504 → 0.00503 on the test split). Negative transfer was observed for naive multi-output trunks, with single-target models performing better. These results provide calibrated predictions suitable for informing instrument selection and acceptance decisions in metrological workflows.

10. Assimilation of Soil Moisture and Ocean Salinity (SMOS) brightness temperature into a large-scale distributed conceptual hydrological model to improve soil moisture predictions: the Murray–Darling basin in Australia as a test case (Hydrology and Earth System Sciences 2020, cites: 9)
   Abstract. The main objective of this study is to investigate how brightness temperature observations from satellite microwave sensors may help to reduce errors and uncertainties in soil moisture and evapotranspiration simulations with a large-scale conceptual hydro-meteorological model. In
addition, this study aims to investigate whether such a conceptual modelling framework, relying on parameter calibration, can reach the performance level of more complex physically based models for
soil moisture simulations at a large scale. We use the ERA-Interim publicly available forcing data set and couple the Community Microwave Emission Modelling (CMEM) platform radiative transfer model with a hydro-meteorological model to enable, therefore, soil moisture, evapotranspiration and brightness temperature simulations over the
Murray–Darling basin in Australia. The hydro-meteorological model is configured using recent developments in the SUPERFLEX framework, which enables tailoring the model structure to the specific needs of the application and to data availability and computational requirements. The hydrological model is first calibrated using only a sample of the Soil Moisture and Ocean Salinity (SMOS) brightness temperature observations (2010–2011). Next, SMOS brightness temperature observations are
sequentially assimilated into the coupled SUPERFLEX–CMEM model (2010–2015). For this experiment, a local ensemble transform Kalman filter is used. Our empirical results show that the SUPERFLEX–CMEM modelling chain is capable of predicting soil moisture at a performance level similar to that obtained for the same study area and with a quasi-identical experimental set-up using the Community Land Model (CLM) . This shows that a simple model, when calibrated using globally and freely available Earth observation data, can yield performance levels similar to those of a physically based (uncalibrated) model. The correlation between simulated and in situ observed soil moisture ranges from 0.62 to 0.72 for the surface and root zone soil moisture. The assimilation of SMOS brightness temperature observations into the SUPERFLEX–CMEM modelling chain improves the correlation between predicted and in situ observed surface and root zone soil moisture by 0.03 on average, showing improvements similar to those obtained using the CLM land surface model. Moreover, at the same time the assimilation improves the correlation between predicted and in situ observed monthly evapotranspiration by 0.02 on average.

1. Backpropagation-Free Test-Time Adaptation for Lightweight EEG-Based Brain-Computer Interfaces (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) face significant deployment challenges due to inter-subject variability, signal non-stationarity, and computational constraints. While test-time adaptation (TTA) mitigates distribution shifts under online data streams without per-use calibration sessions, existing TTA approaches heavily rely on explicitly defined loss objectives that require backpropagation for updating model parameters, which incurs computational overhead, privacy risks, and sensitivity to noisy data streams. This paper proposes Backpropagation-Free Transformations (BFT), a TTA approach for EEG decoding that eliminates such issues. BFT applies multiple sample-wise transformations of knowledge-guided augmentations or approximate Bayesian inference to each test trial, generating multiple prediction scores for a single test sample. A learning-to-rank module enhances the weighting of these predictions, enabling robust aggregation for uncertainty suppression during inference under theoretical justifications. Extensive experiments on five EEG datasets of motor imagery classification and driver drowsiness regression tasks demonstrate the effectiveness, versatility, robustness, and efficiency of BFT. This research enables lightweight plug-and-play BCIs on resource-constrained devices, broadening the real-world deployment of decoding algorithms for EEG-based BCI.

2. Data assimilation in the minerals industry: Real-time updating of spatial models using online production data ( 2018, cites: 1)

response:
Reasoning: The searches reveal no single published paper that combines all three components of SCOPE-Cal: (1) a prototype-based deterministic uncertainty head (like LDU/VOLTA) at the pre-logit layer, (2) a Kalman-filter-style online temperature/bias update weighted by per-sample uncertainty scores, and (3) a rolling weighted conformal layer with uncertainty-bin-stratified thresholds for coverage under shift. Individual components are well-known: LDU-style prototype uncertainty heads exist (Mukhoti et al. 2023), post-hoc calibration under label/covariate shift is studied (Podkopaev & Ramdas 2021), online/test-time calibration exists (UL-TTA, Tent variants), and weighted conformal prediction under shift exists (WQLCP, pseudo-calibrated CP). However, no surfaced paper jointly applies the Kalman-filter-style sequential update of calibration parameters weighted by a prototype uncertainty score, combined with uncertainty-bin-stratified rolling conformal sets, as a unified pipeline for classification calibration under distribution shift. The combination of mechanism families (deterministic uncertainty head + Kalman-style online calibration weighting + stratified conformal layer) has no direct published joint application found in searches, matching the Score 4 anchor.

Prior works:
  - Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty / LDU (Mukhoti et al. 2023): prototype-based deterministic uncertainty head at pre-logit layer — directly overlaps with SCOPE-Cal's uncertainty head component.
  - Distribution-free uncertainty quantification for classification under label shift (Podkopaev & Ramdas, UAI 2021): reweighted conformal prediction and calibration under distribution shift — overlaps with conformal layer component.
  - WQLCP: Weighted Adaptive Conformal Prediction for Robust UQ Under Distribution Shifts (CVPRW 2025): importance-weighted conformal prediction under shift — overlaps with weighted conformal layer.
  - UL-TTA: Ultra-Light Test-Time Adaptation for VLMs (2025): online Bayesian updates of temperature and class priors under shift — overlaps with online calibration component.
  - KOALA: Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2021): Kalman-filter-style parameter updates with EMA noise estimation — overlaps with Kalman-style update mechanism.
  - Coverage Guarantees for Pseudo-Calibrated Conformal Prediction under Distribution Shift (2026): conformal prediction with classifier uncertainty under covariate shift — overlaps with conformal layer.

messages.content:
You are a rigorous research evaluator performing a PAIRWISE comparison
between two proposed methods for the same research query, on the
NOVELTY axis.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Comparison rules
- You see Method A and Method B side-by-side; their condition labels
  are hidden, and their order has been randomized for this comparison.
- Judge novelty by hypothetical literature precedent: which method is
  harder to surface in a Semantic Scholar search? Which has a more
  original combination of mechanisms or introduces a new named
  component / training signal?
- Compare the *strongest novel element* in each method. A method
  with one clearly novel mechanism beats a method with three
  well-established mechanisms.
- DO NOT reward verbosity or technical impressiveness; novelty is
  about non-existence in prior work, not about how detailed the
  method is.
- DO NOT reward problem-method fit (that is the validity axis).

## Output format (no markdown)
  Reasoning: <2-4 sentences identifying the strongest novel element
              in each method, then the comparison>
  Winner: <A | B | tie>

Use "tie" only when the methods are genuinely indistinguishable on
novelty; do not default to "tie" when uncertain.

## Research query
Deep models are overconfident even when wrong, making
downstream decisions unreliable; how can we calibrate
predicted probabilities to reflect actual likelihood,
especially under distribution shift?


## Method A
We propose a three-layer calibration stack that converts raw model scores into shift-robust, coverage-controlled probabilities. Layer 1 learns a post-hoc recalibrator that is explicitly regularized to be smooth and trained with a distributionally robust objective, so it does not overfit a single validation set and remains stable under moderate covariate shift. Layer 2 computes instance-wise confidences by decomposing uncertainty into (a) predictive uncertainty from stochastic predictions and (b) fidelity, i.e., how strongly the model prefers the predicted class over plausible alternatives across perturbations; these are combined into per-class probabilities that better track actual correctness. Layer 3 wraps the calibrated probabilities with an online conformal controller that maintains target coverage (e.g., 1−α top-set coverage) under evolving test distributions by adapting a single threshold on-the-fly and performing a final batch recalibration.

Shift-robust post-hoc recalibration (Layer 1): Fit a lightweight calibrator g(·) on a held-out set using vector/Dirichlet scaling or a small MLP that maps logits to calibrated class probabilities. Constrain g to be gradient-bounded (e.g., parameterize its last layer with a temperature and sigmoid-limited weights, and add a Jacobian-norm penalty) to keep its sensitivity small. Train g with a robust objective that minimizes worst-case expected calibration loss over a χ²-divergence ball around the (optionally reweighted) calibration distribution. In practice, alternate between (i) adversarial reweighting of calibration samples within the χ² radius and (ii) updating g with clipped/penalized gradients to keep the effective Lipschitz constant low. If unlabeled target data are available, learn density ratios and importance-weight the calibration loss so that the robust objective centers on the target covariates.

Uncertainty–fidelity composition (Layer 2): For an input x, generate K stochastic predictions via test-time augmentation, MC dropout, or a small ensemble. Compute normalized predictive entropy Ĥ(x) from the mean softmax. Measure fidelity F_c(x) for each class c as the average top-2 logit margin across the K passes, with a softmax to map margins to [0,1] and temperature tuned on calibration data. Define the per-class calibrated score as s_c(x) = (1 − Ĥ(x)) · F_c(x), then renormalize s over classes to obtain probabilities. When logits are unavailable (e.g., black-box models), approximate F_c via the empirical frequency and consistency of class c across samples, and use normalized sample entropy for (1 − Ĥ). This multiplicative decomposition downweights confident predictions on intrinsically ambiguous inputs and upweights stable, preference-consistent ones.

Coverage control via online conformal calibration (Layer 3): On a split calibration set, compute nonconformity scores A(x, y) = 1 − p_cal(x)[y] using the Layer-1+2 probabilities. Set an initial threshold κ as the (1−α)(n+1)/n quantile (optionally using density-ratio weights to address covariate shift). At test time, output either (a) a probability vector together with a set-valued prediction S(x) = {c : 1 − p_cal(x)[c] ≤ κ} that enjoys 1−α marginal coverage, or (b) a scalar confidence compatible with S by ensuring the true label is in the top-set with probability ≥ 1−α. Update κ online with a decaying step size toward the target coverage based on recent empirical coverage, and periodically perform a batch recalibration on the latest labeled slice. This preserves finite-sample coverage while adapting to drift without retraining the base model.

Practical notes: Train the robust calibrator once per model per domain family; use small K (e.g., 5–10) for Layer 2 to control latency. Evaluate with NLL and ECE (including class- and difficulty-conditional ECE), and verify coverage of S(x) on rolling windows. For safety-critical use, trigger abstention when S(x) is large or when entropy remains high despite recalibration.

## Method B
We propose SCOPE-Cal: a shift-aware calibration pipeline that combines a deterministic uncertainty head, an online adaptive calibrator, and a lightweight conformal layer for risk control. At inference time, the base model produces logits for class probabilities and a companion uncertainty score. The scores are first mapped through a post-hoc calibrator trained offline, then dynamically adjusted online to track distribution shifts. For high-stakes use, we optionally return conformal prediction sets that preserve target coverage even as the data drifts.

Offline, train the task model as usual, then freeze it and attach a prototype-based uncertainty head at the pre-logit latent layer. This head learns a small set of prototypes and outputs a single-pass confidence score in [0,1] indicating how typical the example is with respect to learned class-conditional structure. In parallel, fit a standard multiclass post-hoc calibrator on in-distribution validation data (e.g., vector temperature scaling or a low-rank matrix/bias map) by minimizing NLL; this yields a static mapping g0(z) from logits to calibrated probabilities that is optimal for the source domain.

Online, maintain a tiny stateful calibrator g_t parameterized by a few variables (e.g., global temperature T, optional per-class biases) initialized from g0. Update g_t whenever small batches of labels arrive using a Kalman-filter-like step: treat batch-wise miscalibration statistics (e.g., NLL residuals, reliability within probability bins) as noisy observations of the optimal parameter change and adjust T and biases accordingly. Estimate the observation and process noise with exponential moving averages so the update rate adapts to label scarcity and stochasticity. Weight each example’s contribution by its uncertainty score, giving larger updates for higher-uncertainty samples that are more indicative of shift. When labels are temporarily unavailable, apply a safe, ranking-preserving softening p' = normalize(p^(1 + λ·u)) that reduces confidence proportional to uncertainty u, with λ tuned on source data and adjusted upward if shift detectors flag drift.

For coverage guarantees under shift, run a lightweight, rolling weighted conformal layer on top of g_t. Maintain a small buffer of the most recent labeled examples and compute nonconformity scores (e.g., −log calibrated probability of the true class). Partition the buffer into uncertainty bins and compute per-bin quantile thresholds with optional importance weights from a domain classifier (to partially correct covariate shift). At prediction time, in addition to calibrated probabilities, return a prediction set containing all labels whose nonconformity is below the per-bin threshold for the test point’s uncertainty bin; this preserves approximate coverage overall and within uncertainty strata while keeping sets small on in-distribution inputs.

Which method is more NOVEL?

response:
Reasoning: Method A's strongest novel element is the "uncertainty–fidelity composition" (Layer 2), which introduces a multiplicative decomposition combining predictive entropy with a "fidelity" score (top-2 logit margin stability across stochastic passes) to produce per-class calibrated probabilities. This named mechanism—fidelity as a distinct signal from uncertainty—is a non-trivial framing not commonly found in calibration literature. Method B's strongest novel element is the Kalman-filter-like online update for the calibrator parameters, treating miscalibration statistics as noisy observations and weighting updates by uncertainty scores. While Kalman filtering has been applied in online learning contexts, its specific application here to calibrator parameter adaptation with uncertainty-weighted contributions is moderately novel. However, the prototype-based uncertainty head and per-bin conformal thresholds are relatively established ideas. Comparing the two: Method A's fidelity-entropy multiplicative decomposition as a named calibration mechanism is a more distinctly original framing than Method B's Kalman-filter adaptation, which is a more incremental application of an existing technique to a known problem.

Winner: A

## Definition
Validity = the method's mechanism, as described, would mechanistically
engage with the failure mode that the query identifies. A valid
method's failure-mode-resolving mechanism is traceable: the reader

Validity is NOT:
- novelty (covered separately)
- feasibility (resource/data — not assessed here)
- soundness (gradient flow / dim consistency — not assessed here)

Offline, train the task model as usual, then freeze it and attach a prototype-based uncertainty head at the pre-logit latent layer. This head learns a small set of prototypes and outputs a single-pass confidence score in [0,1] indicating how typical the example is with respect to learned class-conditional structure. In parallel, fit a standard multiclass post-hoc calibrator on in-distribution validation data (e.g., vector temperature scaling or a low-rank matrix/bias map) by minimizing NLL; this yields a static mapping g0(z) from logits to calibrated probabilities that is optimal for the source domain.

Online, maintain a tiny stateful calibrator g_t parameterized by a few variables (e.g., global temperature T, optional per-class biases) initialized from g0. Update g_t whenever small batches of labels arrive using a Kalman-filter-like step: treat batch-wise miscalibration statistics (e.g., NLL residuals, reliability within probability bins) as noisy observations of the optimal parameter change and adjust T and biases accordingly. Estimate the observation and process noise with exponential moving averages so the update rate adapts to label scarcity and stochasticity. Weight each example’s contribution by its uncertainty score, giving larger updates for higher-uncertainty samples that are more indicative of shift. When labels are temporarily unavailable, apply a safe, ranking-preserving softening p' = normalize(p^(1 + λ·u)) that reduces confidence proportional to uncertainty u, with λ tuned on source data and adjusted upward if shift detectors flag drift.

For coverage guarantees under shift, run a lightweight, rolling weighted conformal layer on top of g_t. Maintain a small buffer of the most recent labeled examples and compute nonconformity scores (e.g., −log calibrated probability of the true class). Partition the buffer into uncertainty bins and compute per-bin quantile thresholds with optional importance weights from a domain classifier (to partially correct covariate shift). At prediction time, in addition to calibrated probabilities, return a prediction set containing all labels whose nonconformity is below the per-bin threshold for the test point’s uncertainty bin; this preserves approximate coverage overall and within uncertainty strata while keeping sets small on in-distribution inputs.

Which method has higher VALIDITY (problem-method fit)?

response:
Reasoning: The failure mode is overconfidence in deep models, particularly under distribution shift, requiring calibrated probabilities that reflect actual likelihood. Both methods address this directly through post-hoc calibration, uncertainty quantification, and conformal coverage guarantees. Method A engages the distribution shift problem through a distributionally robust objective with χ²-divergence balls, importance weighting via density ratios, and an online conformal controller that adapts thresholds to evolving distributions. Method B engages it through a Kalman-filter-like online update of calibration parameters, uncertainty-weighted updates prioritizing shift-indicative samples, and a rolling weighted conformal layer with importance weights from a domain classifier. Both trace a clear causal pathway from overconfidence under shift to specific mechanism steps. However, Method A's Layer 1 directly optimizes a worst-case calibration loss over a divergence ball (directly targeting the shift-robustness failure mode at training time), while Method B's offline calibrator is trained on in-distribution data and only adapts online when labels arrive, with a fallback softening heuristic when labels are unavailable. Method A's mechanisms more directly and comprehensively address the calibration-under-shift failure mode at each layer, whereas Method B's core calibrator is initially source-domain-optimal and relies more heavily on online correction.

Winner: A

messages.content:
You are a rigorous research evaluator performing a PAIRWISE comparison
between two proposed methods for the same research query, on the
COHERENCE axis.

## Definition
Coherence = the method, as written, holds together as a technically
valid ML approach. A coherent method:
  - has internally consistent steps (no contradictions, no missing
    prerequisites);
  - combines its proposed mechanisms in compatible ways (not
    paradigm-mixing without explanation);
  - makes assumptions that are achievable given its stated inputs,
    models, and resources;
  - could plausibly be implemented by an ML practitioner without
    having to redesign or invent missing components.

Coherence is NOT:
- novelty (whether the combination is new — separate axis);
- validity (whether the mechanism addresses the query's failure
  mode — separate axis);
- effectiveness (whether the method would empirically win — out of
  scope);
- elegance or technical impressiveness.

## Comparison rules
- You see Method A and Method B side-by-side; their condition labels
  are hidden, and their order has been randomized for this comparison.
- Compare the methods on coherence only. Identify the most serious
  coherence weakness in each (e.g., contradictory step, paradigm
  mismatch, undefined component, infeasible assumption), then judge
  which method is more technically sound overall.
- A method with a single clear coherence flaw is worse than a method
  with no flaws, even if the flawed method is more detailed or
  novel.
- DO NOT reward verbosity or technical impressiveness.
- DO NOT reward problem-method fit (that is the validity axis).

## Output format (no markdown)
  Reasoning: <2-4 sentences identifying the most serious coherence
              weakness (or absence thereof) in each method, then the
              comparison>
  Winner: <A | B | tie>

Use "tie" only when the methods are genuinely indistinguishable on
coherence; do not default to "tie" when uncertain.

## Research query
Deep models are overconfident even when wrong, making
downstream decisions unreliable; how can we calibrate
predicted probabilities to reflect actual likelihood,
especially under distribution shift?


## Method A
We propose a three-layer calibration stack that converts raw model scores into shift-robust, coverage-controlled probabilities. Layer 1 learns a post-hoc recalibrator that is explicitly regularized to be smooth and trained with a distributionally robust objective, so it does not overfit a single validation set and remains stable under moderate covariate shift. Layer 2 computes instance-wise confidences by decomposing uncertainty into (a) predictive uncertainty from stochastic predictions and (b) fidelity, i.e., how strongly the model prefers the predicted class over plausible alternatives across perturbations; these are combined into per-class probabilities that better track actual correctness. Layer 3 wraps the calibrated probabilities with an online conformal controller that maintains target coverage (e.g., 1−α top-set coverage) under evolving test distributions by adapting a single threshold on-the-fly and performing a final batch recalibration.

Shift-robust post-hoc recalibration (Layer 1): Fit a lightweight calibrator g(·) on a held-out set using vector/Dirichlet scaling or a small MLP that maps logits to calibrated class probabilities. Constrain g to be gradient-bounded (e.g., parameterize its last layer with a temperature and sigmoid-limited weights, and add a Jacobian-norm penalty) to keep its sensitivity small. Train g with a robust objective that minimizes worst-case expected calibration loss over a χ²-divergence ball around the (optionally reweighted) calibration distribution. In practice, alternate between (i) adversarial reweighting of calibration samples within the χ² radius and (ii) updating g with clipped/penalized gradients to keep the effective Lipschitz constant low. If unlabeled target data are available, learn density ratios and importance-weight the calibration loss so that the robust objective centers on the target covariates.

Uncertainty–fidelity composition (Layer 2): For an input x, generate K stochastic predictions via test-time augmentation, MC dropout, or a small ensemble. Compute normalized predictive entropy Ĥ(x) from the mean softmax. Measure fidelity F_c(x) for each class c as the average top-2 logit margin across the K passes, with a softmax to map margins to [0,1] and temperature tuned on calibration data. Define the per-class calibrated score as s_c(x) = (1 − Ĥ(x)) · F_c(x), then renormalize s over classes to obtain probabilities. When logits are unavailable (e.g., black-box models), approximate F_c via the empirical frequency and consistency of class c across samples, and use normalized sample entropy for (1 − Ĥ). This multiplicative decomposition downweights confident predictions on intrinsically ambiguous inputs and upweights stable, preference-consistent ones.

Coverage control via online conformal calibration (Layer 3): On a split calibration set, compute nonconformity scores A(x, y) = 1 − p_cal(x)[y] using the Layer-1+2 probabilities. Set an initial threshold κ as the (1−α)(n+1)/n quantile (optionally using density-ratio weights to address covariate shift). At test time, output either (a) a probability vector together with a set-valued prediction S(x) = {c : 1 − p_cal(x)[c] ≤ κ} that enjoys 1−α marginal coverage, or (b) a scalar confidence compatible with S by ensuring the true label is in the top-set with probability ≥ 1−α. Update κ online with a decaying step size toward the target coverage based on recent empirical coverage, and periodically perform a batch recalibration on the latest labeled slice. This preserves finite-sample coverage while adapting to drift without retraining the base model.

Practical notes: Train the robust calibrator once per model per domain family; use small K (e.g., 5–10) for Layer 2 to control latency. Evaluate with NLL and ECE (including class- and difficulty-conditional ECE), and verify coverage of S(x) on rolling windows. For safety-critical use, trigger abstention when S(x) is large or when entropy remains high despite recalibration.

## Method B
We propose SCOPE-Cal: a shift-aware calibration pipeline that combines a deterministic uncertainty head, an online adaptive calibrator, and a lightweight conformal layer for risk control. At inference time, the base model produces logits for class probabilities and a companion uncertainty score. The scores are first mapped through a post-hoc calibrator trained offline, then dynamically adjusted online to track distribution shifts. For high-stakes use, we optionally return conformal prediction sets that preserve target coverage even as the data drifts.

Offline, train the task model as usual, then freeze it and attach a prototype-based uncertainty head at the pre-logit latent layer. This head learns a small set of prototypes and outputs a single-pass confidence score in [0,1] indicating how typical the example is with respect to learned class-conditional structure. In parallel, fit a standard multiclass post-hoc calibrator on in-distribution validation data (e.g., vector temperature scaling or a low-rank matrix/bias map) by minimizing NLL; this yields a static mapping g0(z) from logits to calibrated probabilities that is optimal for the source domain.

Online, maintain a tiny stateful calibrator g_t parameterized by a few variables (e.g., global temperature T, optional per-class biases) initialized from g0. Update g_t whenever small batches of labels arrive using a Kalman-filter-like step: treat batch-wise miscalibration statistics (e.g., NLL residuals, reliability within probability bins) as noisy observations of the optimal parameter change and adjust T and biases accordingly. Estimate the observation and process noise with exponential moving averages so the update rate adapts to label scarcity and stochasticity. Weight each example’s contribution by its uncertainty score, giving larger updates for higher-uncertainty samples that are more indicative of shift. When labels are temporarily unavailable, apply a safe, ranking-preserving softening p' = normalize(p^(1 + λ·u)) that reduces confidence proportional to uncertainty u, with λ tuned on source data and adjusted upward if shift detectors flag drift.

For coverage guarantees under shift, run a lightweight, rolling weighted conformal layer on top of g_t. Maintain a small buffer of the most recent labeled examples and compute nonconformity scores (e.g., −log calibrated probability of the true class). Partition the buffer into uncertainty bins and compute per-bin quantile thresholds with optional importance weights from a domain classifier (to partially correct covariate shift). At prediction time, in addition to calibrated probabilities, return a prediction set containing all labels whose nonconformity is below the per-bin threshold for the test point’s uncertainty bin; this preserves approximate coverage overall and within uncertainty strata while keeping sets small on in-distribution inputs.

Which method is more COHERENT (technically sound and implementable)?

response:
Reasoning: Method A's most serious coherence weakness is in Layer 2, where the uncertainty-fidelity composition multiplies (1 − Ĥ(x)) by F_c(x) and renormalizes. This creates a tension: the renormalization step means the decomposition doesn't actually preserve calibration properties established in Layer 1 — it overwrites the carefully calibrated probabilities from Layer 1 with a new scoring function whose calibration properties are undefined. The three layers are described as a "stack," but Layer 2 effectively discards Layer 1's output and replaces it with a heuristic combination, making the layered architecture internally inconsistent. Method B is more coherent overall: the offline calibrator g0 feeds into the online g_t as initialization, the Kalman-filter-like update is a well-defined mechanism, the uncertainty head integrates naturally with the weighting scheme, and the conformal layer sits cleanly on top of g_t. The one mild weakness in Method B is the label-free softening step p' = normalize(p^(1 + λ·u)), which is a heuristic without clear calibration guarantees, but it is presented as a fallback and doesn't contradict the rest of the pipeline. Overall, Method B's components connect more consistently without internal contradictions.

Winner: B
